g.data-narration: Welcome to OS Debugging. This topic covers essential techniques for finding and fixing errors in operating systems.
Debugging is finding and fixing errors in both hardware and software systems. It involves failure analysis and performance tuning.
g.text: Activity of finding and fixing errors (bugs) in systems
OS debugging: performance issues, crashes, process failures

g.data-narration: OS debugging uses four main approaches: failure analysis, performance monitoring, tracing system calls, and profiling code.
Failure Analysis captures information when processes crash. It uses log files, core dumps, and kernel panic analysis.
g.text: Capturing crash information: log files, core dumps, kernel panic/BSOD

g.data-narration: Log files in /var/log/ store system messages. Key logs include syslog, kern.log, auth.log, and dmesg for diagnostics.
Core dumps save process memory at crash time. Developers use gdb debugger to analyze the call stack and find the bug location.
g.text: gdb commands: backtrace (call stack), info registers, print variable

g.data-narration: When the kernel crashes, Linux shows kernel panic with crash dumps. Windows displays Blue Screen of Death with memory dump files.
Performance monitoring identifies system bottlenecks using tools that measure CPU, memory, disk I/O, and network metrics.
g.text: Tools: top, ps, vmstat, iostat - Measure CPU, Memory, Disk I/O, Network

g.data-narration: Six key metrics: CPU usage, memory usage, disk I/O, network throughput, process status, and load average.
top shows real-time CPU and memory usage per process. ps provides snapshot of all running processes with details.
vmstat reports virtual memory, paging, and CPU activity. iostat shows CPU and device I/O statistics for performance analysis.
g.text: procs r b | memory | swap | io | cpu

g.data-narration: Bottleneck identification uses monitoring tools to find if issues are CPU, memory, I/O, or network bound, then apply fixes.
Tracing records system calls and library calls made by programs. Tools include strace, ltrace, DTrace, and BCC for detailed analysis.
g.text: Recording system calls, library calls - strace, ltrace, DTrace, BCC tools

g.data-narration: strace traces system calls like open, read, write. ltrace traces library calls like printf, malloc for understanding program behavior.
DTrace is a dynamic tracing framework from Solaris tracing kernel and user events. BCC provides eBPF-based Linux tracing tools.
Profiling measures function call frequency and duration to identify performance hotspots in code for optimization.
g.text: Measures function call frequency/duration to find hotspots - gprof, perf, valgrind

g.data-narration: gprof is GNU profiler, perf uses hardware counters, valgrind does memory debugging. These tools identify code hotspots.
g.text: Profiling identifies where programs spend most execution time

g.data-narration: OS debugging combines failure analysis (logs, core dumps), performance monitoring (top, vmstat), tracing (strace), and profiling tools.
Key exam points: log files and core dumps are main failure analysis, gdb backtrace shows call stack, strace vs ltrace difference matters.
g.text: • Core dump analyzed with gdb backtrace shows call stack at crash
• strace = system calls, ltrace = library calls | DTrace from Sun/Solaris | BCC = eBPF tools
